title: The Pin
subtitle: A piece that cannot freely move
summary: Use a line piece to freeze a defender because something more valuable stands behind it.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: The piece in front is tied down
steps.markdown: A [[pin]] happens when a piece cannot move safely because a more valuable piece sits behind it. Here the knight on c6 stands between the bishop and the king.

steps.title: Win the pinned piece
steps.markdown: The knight is pinned to the king. Capture it before it has a chance to escape.

hints.text: The bishop already attacks the knight.
Move the bishop from b5 to c6.

feedback.correct: Right. The pinned knight could not move away without exposing the king, so the bishop wins it cleanly.
feedback.wrong: Use the bishop to capture the piece that is tied to the king.

steps.title: What makes this pin powerful?
steps.markdown: Why was the knight unable to simply move away from the bishop?

options.label: Moving it would expose check on the king
options.explain: Yes. A piece pinned to the king is absolutely pinned because exposing the king is illegal.

options.label: Knights are never allowed to move backward
options.explain: Knights can move in every L-shape direction. The problem was the king behind it.

options.label: The bishop controls every square on the board
options.explain: The bishop controls one diagonal. That diagonal was enough because the king sat behind the knight.

steps.title: Attack the pinned piece again
steps.markdown: A pinned piece often becomes a target. First notice what it is tied to. Then ask whether you can add pressure or simply capture it.
